sentences.text: My name is Eli Benyacar.
And I was-- and I was born in Salonika, Greece.
I was born April 13, 1928.
Benyacar, B-E-N-Y-C-A-R.
And Eli, L-E-- E-L-- E-L-I.
My father's name was Menachem.
My mother was Rena.
And my brother, my oldest brother, was Avraham.
My second-- my next brother was Solomon.
Then I had my sister, Esther, and my little sister, Dora.
Dora.
That consists of seven people in the whole family.
My father had a lumberyard.
He used to buy wooden crates, rework them, and resell them to the different companies for packaging materials.
And we used to be middle-- not rich, not poor, but just comfortable, that I remember.
Prior to that, my father, what I heard-- that we were not that fortunate.
We were quite poor.
But my father was very fortunate that one of his bosses left for Paris.
And he left his yard with two other partners.
And eventually, my father bought out his two partners.
And he became self-- he owned the company by himself.
And after that, that I do remember, we were quite well off.
We were living in Salonika.
And the street of Salonika was [GREEK] 28, which was right in the central of Salonika.
And over there, we used to live in a-- in a five-story building.
We were on the main-- on the main floor.
And-- and I-- I went to school in a-- a Greek school.
I did not go to a Jewish school.
And there, I didn't learn Hebrew.
But we were quite religious.
Not real religious, but we would observe the holidays.
We used to make kiddush every Friday night.
And that's as far as we went.
But--
Friday night was-- it used to be, like, the whole family.
First of all, my mother used to give us a bath.
Everybody.
Me, the-- even my father used to give a bath at-- at home.
The mother was everything.
She used to prepare the-- what do you call it?
The meal, which was a good meal, like Mediterranean meal-- salad, fish.
We didn't have the chicken noodle soup.
Like, we-- we are a different type of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews, let's put it this way.
And we had our own-- the famous meal was beans.
After having the fish and the salad, there was, for Friday night-- was a good-- like cholent, but in a Greek type of cholent.
And we used to have the kiddush.
And-- and there was-- we used to tell many things-- what happened, the whole family.
And then the big brothers used to go out for a good evening.
And of course, I was young at the time.
I used to stay home.
And--
Only in-- on holidays.
Only on Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and-- that's about it.
I never-- I never learned how to daven in Hebrew.
So I-- I did not go to the synagogue.
But my-- my father, he occasionally used to go for mikvah when he used to finish work.
He closed the shop.
He used to-- because our synagogue used to be only about 400 to 500 yards away from our building.
So it was close enough to-- to go there and to do the service for half an hour or so.
The family celebrated Rosh Hashanah--
Oh, we celebrated it.
Pesach, the eight days of Pesach.
We used to have exactly the same as, a matter of fact, we do here in-- in Canada, Pesach.
We used to do the same thing in Greece.
But ours used to be in Ladino-- Spanish, you know?
But it's the same meaning.
In Salonika, we used to be close to 50,000 Jews.
And there were--
I would say that 65% or 75% were poor Jews.
Poor-- there weren't very many rich [?
Jews. ?] But who was rich, it was rich.
But there-- there was an awful lot of poor Jewish people, which-- and some, they were middle class.
But those middle class used to work in stevedores in the port.
And they were making good money.
You know, they were [? traders, ?] I call it, or stevedores, unloading the boats.
They would specialize.
The Greek Jews would specialize in-- what do you call it?
In unloading boats.
And stevedores, they-- they own, actually-- the whole-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the whole group, it used to be strictly Jewish people.
They were strong, like mules.
Real strong.
And they were-- they were able to do that.
But the rest of them?
We used to have a lot of sections of poor Jews, which they were-- the Jewish community used to help them.
Like Pesach.
Used to come in.
Used to give [INAUDIBLE] for nothing.
And we used to have-- a lot of organizations used to help the people.

Like, my father had a couple of workers in the-- we used to take them-- Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur, take them to the store, buy things for the kids, buy some different things, because could not afford it.
A worker was strictly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] six loaves of bread a day, the-- the salary they used to get, you know?
This is-- the amount of money was to get, you could only buy six or seven loaves of bread, and that was it.
But you only needed one loaf of bread or two loaf of bread, so the rest of the money was-- bought food they would grow in Greece, like a lot of vegetables and so on.
And they managed.
But when it comes to clothing, they had to need a helping hand.
And my father used to take them and-- to the big people they used to do business with, and used to buy certain things.
But as far as I was concerned, we used to be well-- quite well off.
We used to go to buy good clothes for Pesach and Yom-- you know, and the holidays.
And as matter of fact, we were quite well.
My brother, and my oldest brother, and my sister, they used to have camel-hair coats.
You know, they were-- they were really, really up to date.
They had a sewing machine at home.
We had everything.
You name it, we had it, till this-- the war started in 1948-- in 1940, October of '40, which with the Italians, were the first people who attacked us in Greece.
And then we didn't have not much problem.
I was, at the time, at the sixth grade of public school, which actually was second grade of high school.
I went from the fourth public school to the first high school.
I got-- instead of having six years of public and six years of high, was four of public and two-- and eight in-- in high school.
Yes.
[INAUDIBLE] so happened, I was-- we only were two Jewish kids in the whole school.
The school that I did go-- was only two Jewish boys.
The rest of them were all-- because we were near my house where I used to live.
And--
I would say 80% were in communities.
I would say 80% were in communities.
And the--
Where I used to live, I would say 80%. 80%, but were at the upper-- upper-class Jews, you see?
Because where I used to live was a lot of five and six-story buildings.
And they have apartments.
And there was a few Jews living there.
But right across, there was already sort of a group that were poor people living in small, like, sheds, like a small, one-floor, made-of-wood, you know, houses.
And they were living, you know, very good down there, because in Greece, the weather wasn't that cold and, you know, there had to be furnaces and everything.
[INAUDIBLE] we didn't have-- we didn't need that central heating or anything like that, so-- and--
Yes, I had non-Jewish friends and Jewish friends.
I had both.
I had both, because--
With the-- with the gentile friends, [INAUDIBLE] I happened to have a couple friends.
They were-- were across from my house, with the bank of-- the [?
Farmer's ?] Bank, a big bank.
And I had to-- the manager of the bank had two sons.
And I was very close with them, because I was in the same school.
And I used to go to his house upstairs-- they used to live in the bank upstairs-- and do homework.
And I was very close, because I spoke Greek fluently.
And I--
No.
At home was Jewish.
It was Spanish.
[INAUDIBLE] instead of Yiddish, was Spanish, because we are Sephardic Jews.
But I learned-- I was-- learned Greek the same as a Greek, you know?
And I was-- with the rest of people, the rest of the friends that I had, those two brothers, we'd talk, certainly, Greek.
But I had some Jewish neighbors, you know, that were also close friends, you know?
Not in school.
But mind you, in the-- in the fifth and the-- when I went to the fifth and the sixth years-- what do you call-- already, I went to the high school, and had friends who were Jewish friends in there.
It was more than-- than me being Jewish there.
It was a lot of Jewish.
And I had some Jewish friends, which was in the high school.
But up to the [? four ?] public school was only-- only was-- we were only two Jewish boys there.
No.
No whatsoever, except the fourth year of public school, which was-- I believe it was in 1938-- came a new prime minister.
We had a king always in Greece.
Always was a king.
But used to have prime ministers and-- which was Metaxas.
His name was Metaxas.
And he was a-- was an anti-Semite.
And he started building the Hitler Young group in schools-- young, making parades and so on.
And I happened to be in the school that nobody knew that I was Jew-- I mean, they knew.
The-- the school principal knew I was Jewish.
But they didn't care, because I was-- I was a student.
And then once-- one time, one day, he came in.
They were checking the records of everybody in school.
They found out my name, Benyacar.
And they found out that I was Jewish, so they kicked me out of the-- of the group.
But I was still in school.
But I could not participate in their meetings and-- where I used to be before.
And that's about the-- what happened when I was up to the fourth grade.
No one--
Nothing special.
Strictly-- it doesn't make sense, you know?
It was-- it wasn't hatred.
It was not teaching hatred.
There was-- but they were teaching discipline more than hatred.
And there was no-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] you could say there might have been a little bit of an anti-Semite [INAUDIBLE] in Greece, in Salonika.
But you don't-- you didn't feel it.
When you walk on the street, you didn't care if you were a Jew or a gentile.
Nobody will say you're a dirty Jew or something like that.
We didn't have that when I was growing up, anyway.
So--
Well, when I was [INAUDIBLE].
It didn't mean anything to me.
I was young.
It didn't mean anything.
I was only, what? 12 years old, 11 years old.
What-- what did I know at the time?
But in the meantime, I belonged to the Boy Scouts, the Jewish Boy Scouts, in the same time, because you're used to having a different group.
And it didn't mean anything.
We used to go parade with the Jewish scouts, the Jew-- I used to have the uniform and everything else.
But doesn't-- it didn't mean very much, because we never thought in my age-- never thought too much of-- of the Semites and everything like that.
It was-- first of all, I was mixed up with gentile friends and I was mixed up with Jewish friends, and I didn't feel it.
I didn't-- it might have been a little bit of anti-Semite [? culture, ?] you know?
And ?] every country is.
But not to the extent from what I hear today from my friends in-- in Toronto.
The friends that I do have.
I don't have no Greek friends, only a Polish and Hungarians, and Lithuanians.
And we get together.
And they tell me these horror stories that they had at home.
We didn't have that.
No way.
We did not have this kind of a thing.
And I felt free.
I-- we-- were were free till-- all the time, till the war broke out, you know?
Till the-- Did you have a bar mitzvah?
No, because at the time, the Germans came in, you see?
I just happened to be 13.
I was born in '28.
And in '41, they came in.
And I didn't have no bar mitzvah.
And on top of that, I didn't go to chader.
So even if I would have had a bar mitzvah, would have been just called to the seder, to the [? thing, ?] and say just small thing, and-- and that was it.
But I did not have a bar mitzvah.
My two brothers, they did have a bar mitzvah.
And they belonged to the [? core, ?] you know?
They used to-- we used to-- in the shul, they used to belong to the [? core. ?] They both were very well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they went to Jewish schools.
And they knew the-- they know how to daven.
They [INAUDIBLE] very, very well, as a matter-- my two brothers.
Not me.
Me, I didn't.
I didn't have the opportunity, because when-- when my two brothers were growing up, we were living in a Jewish section with a poor-- we-- we were poor.
They were grown-- grown up at that time, because [? we are-- ?] it was a big difference between my two brothers and me in age.
We were-- [? it ?] was 14 years with my-- my oldest brother and 11 years my second oldest brother.
That's quite a bit of difference between.
I had very little in common with them, you see.
But they-- in-- in Salonika, they went to Jewish schools.
They learned Hebrew.
They were called from many houses to light the candles for-- what do you call it?
For Hanukkah or many things to do for a lot of Jewish people that didn't know how to read or daven.
And they were doing this kind of a chore-- chores.
But not me.
This was the-- at the time as we were strictly living in a Jewish ghetto.
Actually, you could call it, like, a Jewish ghetto with-- before the war.
Strictly Jewish people over there.
I'll tell you the truth.
I had no dreams whatsoever.
My dreams was that I was sort of manipulator.
I always liked to make money.
I always found to make [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] what do you call?
Bingos?
Collect a few kids in it and grab the $0.50 here, $0.50 there.
And then I used to have the-- make the puppets, you know?
Collect a few people.
And I used to buy those books with different characters.
And I used to have a big sheet of-- there, with a light.
And I used to play the things.
And I used to be sort of a-- always had to do something to make a buck.
And that helped me when the Germans came in.
When the Germans came in, I was about 13 years old.
And not too long-- within three months-- first of all, my brother, oldest brother, was an accountant, was working for my father.
My second brother, Solomon, he was a radio mechanic and a typewriter mechanic.
So when the Germans came in, they took all the radios out.
No more radios available.
So he was out a job.
So-- and then they took my father's yard.
So my father was-- was out of-- nothing.
And my oldest brother, there was nothing.
We had quite a bit of money saved up.
But since my-- my father was illiterate.
He was not educated.
They never believed in banks.
We had a lot of money.
But we had them all at home in paper, not in gold.
If-- we weren't-- they weren't that smart to make the-- the-- the paper currency into gold.
Then we would have been on top of the world.
But they kept their old piles of big denominations of-- of-- of drachmas into the house.

And as a matter of fact, when we used to go out in the evening, even before the war, my mother had to carry a-- a big bag of money with her when we'd go out for dinner, you know, because we didn't believe in banks.
And you don't want to leave it at home, because they might rob you.
Well, my two brothers, they were-- and my-- my sister was younger, [? though. ?] My two brothers used to have apres-midis.
Apres-midi is a French word, a Spanish word.
They used to get together at a house.
They-- they would-- what do you call it?
They would get a band and have a party at home, you know?
They'd bring their own food.
Or they had their own clubs, the Jewish clubs, which we used to go-- boys and, you know-- and younger people of 20 to 18, 19, 20, 21.
They used to-- a lot of activities there, which I [INAUDIBLE] because I was young.
And they-- they-- they used to belong to the club, the Jewish clubs, which they were-- my oldest brother was strictly associated with Jewish people, Jewish [INAUDIBLE].
My second oldest, it was mixture.
He used to be with the Jewish friends, and also used to have a lot of gentile friends, which became very, very helpful when the Germans came in.
But as far as my sister, she was [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] a homemade girl.
She used to knit, you know, and all that.
She-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] she hardly went out with boys.
Finally, she met a handsome boy, like a Jewish boy.
And they used to go for a walk.
And my mother would send me behind the streets to see what they're doing, you know?
Spy on them.
And I used to [? hide from ?] one street to-- and they used to sit holding hands.
And then he used to bring her home.
And this was just during the war.
This was the 1941-- '41, '42.
They never got married.
None of my brothers or sisters got married during the-- while we were alive.
None whatsoever.
Nothing about Germany.
We were dummies.
We were just a bunch of dummies.
And we could really not think we--
There came in a few German Jews.
They were expelled from Germany.
As a matter of fact, a few-- two German Jews came in 1936, where my brother was a radio technician there.
And it was from Blaupunkt.
This is the German radio company.
Blaupunkt is a very famous radio manufacturer in-- in-- in-- Germany.
And where my brother used to work, used to have the agency for this company, for-- for the Greece.
And those two Jews-- German Jews came in.
They were working at the factory in Germany.
So they came in and-- but did they say they expelled them from Germany?
No.
At the time, it wasn't that.
It wasn't the-- what do you call it?
Kristallnacht yet.
But they-- they left.
So they came.
But we could never believe it, what's happening, you know?
We never thought of it-- something like this would happen eventually.
And we were, till-- till October 1940, we were not-- we're not aware of what's happening in [?
Europe. ?] Even the war started in 1939, in Poland, in September '39, in Poland-- but we were not aware.
As a matter of fact, we were not even aware there existed any other type of Jews.
We-- we didn't expect-- we didn't even know a Ashkenazi Jew, especially me.
And maybe a lot of people would.
But really, we didn't really know about the-- the Ashkenazi Jews.
We were strictly Sephardic Jews at home.
And we found out later, as--
October 1940, the-- Italy went to war with Greece.
That's-- that's it.
That was the end of my schooling days.
You know?
And they started bombing them.
And so what happened, Greece was advancing, even Italy was such a big country.
But Greece was advancing throughout Albania.
They were taking a lot of territory, you know?
And then Italy became-- what do you call it?
Became allies with the Germans.
So of course, the Germans started coming through Yugoslavia-- you know, starting in Yugoslavia and Serbia.
And they came through Greece.
So-- and of-- Do you remember the day the Germans came into Greece?
It was around-- I believe it was around April in '41.
I don't know exactly the date.
But it was in-- around--
Just-- just waiting for them.
We were just waiting.
We wanted to see the parade, you know, as they were coming in.
We never thought-- we never felt it could do anything to us, you know?
We were out on the street.
And we see those tanks.
And they-- they were marching in.
And, I mean, we never [? thought ?] anything.
We just-- we went outside to see them. coming in, that's all.
And that was in-- in what do you call it?
In-- in-- in '41 already.
But in-- in-- in '40, we-- we were winning the war.
So when the Germans became allies with Italians-- so the-- the Italians came in and occupied certain part of Greece.
The Germans came in and picked up [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] Athens, Salonika.
But when it came to the [?
Cyclades, ?] which is the islands, the Italians occupied there, because they were allied.
So they-- and then they kept it for a while.
They never bothered being, you know, who's Jewish or not Jewish.
They-- they-- they kept it cool, you know, for the few-- first few months.
They kept it cool.
I believe it was [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] August, maybe July-August of '41, already they starting.
They asked all the Jews from age 16 to 45 to-- to gather in-- to gather into [GREEK], which is the-- one of the famous-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you call [INAUDIBLE] in-- in English?
Like a big park, you know?
And they concentrate quite a few Jews down there.
And everybody had to register their names-- you know, the people who went over there.
Whoever went, you had to register their names.
But of course, I was young, so I didn't-- I was outside this.
[INAUDIBLE] seen them [INAUDIBLE] the [? star, ?] some of the [? hitting ?] of them and so on, which I never saw that before.
It was the-- just the beginning.
Well, one of my brothers was taken solo.
They took him to labor in-- inside Greece.
My oldest brother, we paid a Greek officer to be his maid.
Like, his-- his assistant, you know?
Like a Greek-- but it wasn't doing nothing, actually.
But we-- we was a friend of a doctor, of a gentile doctor, which was very close to our family.
So we-- they-- they paid him quite a bit.
And my oldest brother did not-- my oldest brother did not go to the work.
So my brother, my younger one, my-- Solomon, he want for about, I don't know.
Maybe six weeks or eight weeks.
He saw the tyranny there-- over there, so he ran away.
He came home full of lice and all that, dirty and so on.
Never went back.
Because also, my brother spoke good Greek.
He was also-- they couldn't tell him-- my whole family didn't look like Jewish, anyway.
They looked-- Knowing what was happening, did your family attempt to leave?
Where?
This was only the-- it's only the beginning.
That was only the beginning.
We never said, well, that's OK.
That's the beginning.
As I told you, we were ignorant.
We were very ignorant.
We never dreamed-- well, we never knew what going on on the other side of the world, you know?
We never knew what was going on.
[INAUDIBLE] to help?
What [? is there ?] to help?
They didn't do us any harm.
And, as I'll come later, some of the neighbors did help, you know?
And some, they did not help.
Some were waiting-- waiting for you to move out of this house and to grab whatever is left over, you know?
But at that time, just the beginning of start getting together the Jews.

And all of a sudden, they [INAUDIBLE] we start putting the star of David and start taking Greek Jews, make them into couples to put a big [? band ?] to start gathering-- they had a system how to do it.

But-- but we were greenhorns, so-- As we were living at the-- this big apartment there-- for a while, we were-- we were there almost-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] I would say until the beginning of '43, '43.
And-- and middle of '42, they didn't bother us.
And they started-- started making different groups, like the [INAUDIBLE] ghettos.
They started taking, first of all, sections from north, where were the-- the poor Jews, you [? know?
Over ?] there.
They started concentrating those, bringing them into a ghetto-- like, same group.
And then--
Yeah.
Oh, OK.
